title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved minutes, acknowledged permits, discussed CIP updates including a new $780,000 LRIP grant for Birch Street reconstruction, and addressed sanitary sewer discharge violations by directing staff to create enforcement language with fines of $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road ($94,860) and awarded contracts for recycled asphalt crushing and construction services.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Weston Water Utility PSC annual report
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue duplex
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelinek and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Program (LRIP) grant of approximately $780,000 for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project will likely move to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue settlement repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating, and Schofield Avenue punch list items with HOS Construction including manhole repairs and concrete work.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th along with Marathon County. Crews completed mailbox repairs, sign repairs, spring sweeping, and hot mix patching. Significant dewatering was required near Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week due to flooding. Storm damage from East Nick area required tree cleanup. Culverts failed on Jones Street requiring installation of a third 18-inch pipe. Vehicle maintenance included removing plow equipment and applying anti-rust treatment.

Josh reported on clear water infiltration issues, noting April treated 53-54 million gallons while only 37 million was pumped into the system - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. Investigation found sump pumps illegally connected to sanitary sewers at smaller lift stations like Kathleen. Staff discussed the upcoming INI study with in-pipe technologies to identify infiltration sources. The Carrie well rehabilitation started today with plans for tower cleaning.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. Utility value reached nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service including wells 7 and 8 and PFAS-related improvements. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers: 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael presented a League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supporting long-term transportation funding solutions. He noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and Wisconsin is affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership. The committee voted unanimously to recommend the village board adopt Resolution 2026-014.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material at the Ryan Street site, totaling $126,900. Analysis showed the village's cost of $17.11/cubic yard compares to Wausau's $28/cubic yard, saving approximately $130,000 across this year's three projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Committee discussed enforcement of illegal sump pump connections to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee members distinguished between unknowing and willful violations. After discussion about waiting for the INI study results to identify problem areas, the committee voted unanimously to direct staff to create enforcement language with fines of $100/day for violations.

Michael explained a proposed duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue needs temporary connection to existing water/sewer mains since mains don't currently extend to the lot. When Radkey Avenue is reconstructed, the owner will pay assessment for main extension. Committee requested adding specific timeline language. Motion passed unanimously to recommend the agreement with reconstruction no later than 2029.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Hellin Street. Rothschild holds the contract with Steam Construction; Weston is responsible for water/sewer work totaling $94,860. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Construction Services for Alderson/Jelinek and Bladel Projects
discussions.body: Michael presented construction inspection proposals received Friday. For Alderson/Jelinek roundabout: MSA at $37,315 vs Clark Dietz at $48,910 - MSA recommended. For Bladel Avenue: MSA at $70,400 vs Clark Dietz at $73,730 - Clark Dietz recommended despite slightly higher cost due to having full-time experienced staff versus MSA's summer intern. Motion passed unanimously for both recommendations.

actionItems: Move Birch Street reconstruction from 2029 to 2028 using LRIP grant funding
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines
Enter water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline of 2029
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860
Award recycled asphalt crushing to King Gravel at $4.23/ton ($126,900 estimated)
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout construction services ($37,315)
Approve Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue construction services ($73,730)
Village board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting transportation funding
Send water main flushing notices to residents - flushing to begin next week
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs Wednesday/Thursday this week
Schedule special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work product transmittals, educational presentations, reports
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: The commission elected Trustee Erling as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated and elected as Vice Chair with Jackson providing the second. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously by voice vote.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee. Passed unanimously by voice vote.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. He reported approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year and announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year despite the same $5,000 request as 2024. The commission requested better tracking of hotel room usage for future reports. Motion to approve $5,000 grant was made by the Chair, seconded by Jackson, and passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting winner Anastasia was at her table. The candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese and were impressed with the community. This was noted as the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, subject to staff conditions. The commission also approved a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts, establishing twice-yearly inspections to protect the village's drinking water supply.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff discussion

discussions.item: Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Chief Jeremy explained challenges including a gas pipeline easement that required rotating the building orientation to face east. The building is designed for 50-60 officer capacity for future growth beyond current 40 officers and 4 clerical staff. An alternate storage shed was discussed, with Commissioner Zagami suggesting relocating it further north to accommodate future building expansion. The landscaping meets current relaxed code standards. Motion to approve was made and passed unanimously with conditions 2-5 as listed by staff.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed a twice-annual inspection program for salt storage facilities within the wellhead protection district - a warm weather inspection for structural integrity and a winter operational inspection for material handling practices. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg that never went through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed it should be brought into compliance. Discussion emphasized the village's vulnerability due to shallow wells (90-110 ft) and sandy soil. Motion to approve passed unanimously, with amendment to report back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee.
Motion to approve minutes as presented was made, seconded, and passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites.

Electric vehicle charging stations will be added to a future agenda, likely in the fall. Discussion arose about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing. Staff mentioned Ehlers has prepared a presentation on residential TIF qualifications that could be shared with commissioners, possibly integrated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He asked the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping standards for the police department project, referencing previous relaxation of requirements for the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on visits and findings to ensure accountability.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation in wellhead protection district
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send calendar invite for June 1st steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging stations to be added to future agenda in fall
Staff to share Ehlers presentation on residential TIF qualifications with commissioners

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on besides approving the April 1st meeting minutes. The meeting focused on introducing department missions, budgets, and current initiatives including homelessness response, veteran services, and nutrition programs.

agenda.item: Call to order and attendance (Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg attending remotely)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute, ordinance, resolution or policy
Review of standing committee ordinances including missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.item: Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting the public. She highlighted 1,200+ licenses overseen, a new homeless system response position funded through partnership with City of Wausau, public health vending machines stocked with free resources including tick kits, and the Nurse Family Partnership program now graduating its first two-year-olds. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE with about half the $5 million budget from tax levy. Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness, and Suginaka confirmed plans are in place with staff trained in emergency response from COVID experience.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. He described the department's divisions including child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, and economic/child support services with about 140 FTE. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program that addresses root causes of housing instability, noting many participants had never had a credit account, bank account, or formal lease. The department has a $22 million budget with approximately $7 million from tax levy.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported the office now has 3.0 FTE and had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900 projected. He noted $44 million in annual benefits flowing to local veterans and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include working with Center for Veterans Issues on veteran housing, suicide prevention efforts (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans), and helping surviving spouses with dependency and indemnity compensation benefits of up to $2,400/month. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional cooperation, and Galindo confirmed good working relationships with neighboring counties while noting state statute requires each county to have its own VSO.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) as an integrated organization providing aging and disability services. He noted significant financial challenges with nutrition costs up 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat due to Older Americans Act not being reauthorized since 2024. The organization has about 60 FTEs plus 385-400 volunteers. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate nutrition program at the Hmong American Center.
The agenda listed May 29th but Vice Chair Rosenberg and Supervisor Kremes caught that this was incorrect. The committee confirmed the next meeting would be Wednesday, June 3rd at 3 PM.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested agenda items for future meetings
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion to upcoming meetings
Next meeting scheduled for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3 PM

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bikes
Committee appointments and farmers market discussion

discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented that the village lacks detailed data on actual park maintenance costs. Trustee Barb spoke in support, noting the fees help new development pay for park infrastructure. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passed unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passed unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional agreement with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, Riverside and others where one fire inspection sticker from any participating municipality is valid across all communities, eliminating redundant inspections for food trucks. Ordinance 26-008 was approved with motion by Lope, second by Zagami, passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the policy regarding when trustees can attend meetings remotely. After discussion about not wanting chairs to judge whether reasons for remote attendance are acceptable, the board voted to strike sentences requiring health/safety/welfare justification. Amendment by Beck, second by Clark, passed 5-2 with Hermling and Lob Sra opposed. The original motion as amended also passed 5-2 with the same dissenting votes.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Discussion-only item addressed concerns about the farmers market's new location after losing the Camp Phillips/Schofield site when the property owner developed it. Trustee Lisa raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass in Kennedy Park. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors now have their vehicles with them. Multiple trustees discussed that Mont Miller Park would be too remote and traffic would decline. No action was taken; the board acknowledged this is an evolving situation.
The president appointed Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, passed unanimously. Openings remain on Parks, HR/Finance, and one more on CLIPS.

Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street was approved for secondhand article and jewelry dealer licenses, contingent on satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Motion by Hermling, second by Clark, with amendment for contingencies, passed unanimously.

During public works report, Michael Dolan explained the village has advertised for an engineering tech position multiple times but the specific skill set is scarce in central Wisconsin. Even consulting firms are pulling staff from southeastern Wisconsin. This relates to the $108,000 being spent on outside engineering consultants that Jim Pitel criticized during public comment.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment criticizing the $108,000 being spent on outside engineering consultants when the village should fill the vacant engineering technician position. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a SAFER meeting before being formally appointed by the board. He called for more transparency and accountability from the administrator, and criticized the lack of a detailed Kennedy Park funding report in the meeting packet.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with ordinance 26-010 and resolution 2026-015
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Comprehensive plan steering committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 PM
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspection assistance
Speed study on Weston Avenue to be completed and brought back to the board
Committee applications to be included in board meeting packets going forward
Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels appointed to CLIPS committee
Electronics drive date corrected to June 6 (not May 20)
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove health/safety/welfare justification requirements

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held an organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the at-large Executive Committee positions, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe tornado and flood damage in Ringle and other communities. The fee reduction from $65 to approximately $36.94 per ton will help residents dispose of storm debris through June 17th.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
Vote on suspension of rules for tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, noting goals included increasing diversity of perspectives, allowing supervisors to grow as leaders, and accommodating supervisor preferences. He noted over 34% of the board is in its first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Kremes, was carried but not unanimous. Supervisor Gao noted the system marked him absent but did not allow him to vote.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained she nominated Supervisor Leur because she had seen her advocate for the county's youngest children and oldest elders. After paper ballot voting, Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to Landfill Tipping Fees
discussions.body: County Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for disaster debris following severe tornado and flood damage. The standard $65/ton fee would be reduced to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris, with the state waiving $13.10 and the Town of Ringle waiving $2.33. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, requesting the county waive all fees and extend the timeline beyond 60 days. Supervisor Robinson suggested tracking costs and potentially reimbursing the Community Foundation retroactively. The board voted unanimously to suspend rules to hear the resolution, then voted unanimously to approve Resolution 26-26.
Items C10 through C14A were approved unanimously on a motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P and seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty asked the county to waive all fees for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their fees, and that private businesses have donated significant resources. She stated one business has incurred $40,000 in cleanup expenses. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents with demolished homes, requesting fee waivers for underinsured residents and extended timelines since many insurance adjusters have not yet visited affected properties.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large Executive Committee positions
Resolution 26-26 approved unanimously reducing disaster debris tipping fees to approximately $36.94/ton through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding unreimbursed disaster costs
Solid waste management board to bring back a clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Staff directed to track disaster debris costs for potential future review

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and forwarded an amended fee waiver resolution to the county board. The committee voted to waive only reconnect fees (not other zoning or pouts fees) for properties impacted by the April 17, 2026 tornado, rather than the broader fee waiver originally proposed by staff.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing - Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Department overview - Parks, Recreation and Forestry (Tom Lovelin)
Department overview - Conservation, Planning and Zoning (Lori Mskimmons)

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pago presented a staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for or against. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and the town's approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pago presented a petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy designation; Pago explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make the existing structures conforming uses. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and pouts fees for properties damaged by the tornado. Supervisor Drew questioned fairness of helping only declared disaster areas. Supervisor Leur asked about revenue impact and staff costs. Supervisor Drebeck raised concerns about consistency and noted the recent fee increase for pouts systems. After debate, Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution; Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then proposed an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees ($160), excluding all other zoning and pouts fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment passed. The amended resolution then passed but was noted as not unanimous.

Director Dave Hagenbusher provided an educational presentation covering the department's 16-year history, operations of the landfill serving Marathon, Portage and Shawano counties, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy launched in September 2025, and upcoming challenges including leachate treatment requirements by 2032. The department processes about 1,000 tons daily with 11 FTEs.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie (who had an emergency). He covered the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal positions, operation of both county and city park systems since 1924, and the 30,700-acre county forest. A Nine Mile County Forest study showed out-of-town visitors generate $3.25 million for the local economy. Lovelin announced his retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) to county board for April 17, 2026 tornado-impacted properties
Forward James Offer/David Tract rezoning approval to county board
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning approval to county board
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
Tom Lovelin retiring June 1st; Joe Tucker selected as new County Forest Administrator

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of new kayak launch signs for the Eau Claire River Trail and deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center to next month. The meeting featured public comment opposing the dog day proposal and questioning Kennedy Park fundraising transparency, with staff providing updates on the ongoing master plan timeline.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Future agenda items and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes were approved with a motion and second. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Staff presented a timeline document showing the project history from the comprehensive outdoor recreation plan approval in November 2023 through the recent ribbon cutting on May 9th. Director Osterbring explained the village planned to contribute approximately $7 million of the estimated $14.3 million total project cost for infrastructure. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about funding timelines, and staff confirmed there are no current village capital dollars earmarked for Kennedy Park projects as those were pulled pending the master plan. The Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for field lights, having raised approximately $84-85,000 of the $117,000 goal, with a public check presentation planned for their June tournament.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing the approximate locations of fallen trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them from equipment. Dan explained the mat installation process and noted the mats are village-owned with extras available for future trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the next meeting because Chair Katrina had an unforeseen issue preventing her attendance. The resident who spoke during public comment opposing the idea was told his objection was noted and he could return next month.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges in removing graffiti from concrete due to limited equipment access - the pressure washer cannot reach the trail locations due to steep ditches and narrow bridges. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, sealer applications, and potential volunteer workdays. Committee members expressed interest in exploring volunteer opportunities similar to how memorial benches were originally installed via a director team-building day. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan from Marathon County presented version 7 of proposed kayak launch signs for the Eau Claire River. Signs will be installed at launches 3 (Highway J), 4 (Ross), and 5 (Yellow Banks) within Weston, plus locations in Schofield. The signs include a map, QR code linking to river gauge data, and location-specific history sections. Funding comes from Marathon County Parks Foundation and state waterway grants at no cost to the village. The committee approved a motion to install the signs and forward to the village board for approval. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: Two people provided public comment. First, a resident from 6504 [address unclear] spoke in opposition to allowing dogs at the aquatic center, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period. I like dogs, but it's not a place for them.' Second, Jim Pensan from 5002 [Street] Weston spoke remotely, criticizing the 4:00 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation, and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the $8,700 figure for baseball equipment 'too coincidental' and asking where money from the glow event and other fundraising efforts is being tracked. He stated the numbers presented are 'extremely convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Approved installation of Yellow Banks kayak launch signs - forward to village board for approval
Deferred Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti removal options and possible anti-vandalism coatings for Eau Claire River Trail
Staff to bring food allergy awareness playground signage proposal to future meeting for discussion
Mock Miller master plan process kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 p.m. in training room

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee held a routine meeting focused on acknowledging April budget and financial reports, and received an educational presentation on the Municipal Clerk's office operations. Committee member Evan Soaper announced his resignation as he is moving away from Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and operations
Staff remarks and committee member announcements

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April financial reports (budget status, TIFF details, legal details)
discussions.body: Jessica presented the April reports. Regarding TIFF one closure, she explained the bond payoff is anticipated for October-November, requiring a CDA resolution to call the bonds. The process involves notifying US Associate Trust and DTC. A committee member confirmed the final numbers still look good and the village will be able to pay off the debt. Motion to acknowledge items five, six, and seven was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented a comprehensive overview of the clerk's office operations, staffing (reduced from four full-time to two full-time and one part-time), election administration, licensing, tax collection, and pet licensing. Notable points included: 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is done at the clerk's office counter; pet licensing fees increased from $10 to $20 per the county's request to help fund the Humane Society; the office processed over 800 voter registrations in the November 2024 presidential election; and the quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing. Discussion included concerns about non-dog stray contract costs ($175 per mouse was cited as an example) and whether newsletter costs could be reduced.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well. She has been present for 6-7 hours each of the last three Saturdays helping vendors. Tuesday markets have faced weather challenges. The market has expanded to include food trucks, coffee vendors, and craft vendors. Produce vendors will increase mid to end of June. A committee member reported positive feedback from vendors about the setup and location. Concerns were raised about parking on Alderson and Newport streets, with clarification that only west side parking on Alderson is now allowed while east side remains no parking.

Evan Soaper announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted there are now open spots on this committee and others, encouraging outreach to interested citizens.

actionItems: Minutes from previous meeting approved
April budget status report, TIFF one and two detail reports, and legal details report acknowledged
CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIFF one bonds for October payoff
Chief election inspector pay increase to $15/hour to be proposed for next election term
Recruit citizens to fill open committee positions including Evan Soaper's vacancy
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee advanced several ordinances and addressed code enforcement matters at this May 4th meeting. Key actions included recommending approval of nuisance code amendments, an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on emotos, mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, and a raze order for a condemned property on Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed but deferred action on overnight parking regulations.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Department reports - Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Code Enforcement Officer Roman reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work with zoning violations during summer for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Reports were acknowledged unanimously on motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Olsen.

The committee reviewed final amendments to Chapter 50, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways) with language that properties must comply with jurisdiction having authority if different. Motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees made by Olsen, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred regarding the model ordinance for e-bikes. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bike-type vehicles) on roadways, sidewalks, and trails, citing safety concerns about unlicensed vehicles without lights or turn signals. Committee member Mike raised concerns about electric scooters used by middle school children. The committee agreed to add emoto restrictions to roadways while leaving officer discretion for children's play scooters. Rothschild has not yet approved the ordinance; Schofield declined as unnecessary. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property, seconded by Sherwanka. Passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (most have 5-hour windows from approximately 2am-7am). Michael from Public Works noted plowing rarely occurs before 4-5am and suggested eliminating overnight parking restrictions while signing 6-8 specific problem areas. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions for officer enforcement time. Committee deferred action, directing Michael to identify specific exception areas and return in July with recommendations.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest High School parking lot where parishioners park during services. Michael estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000, while a lighted system would cost $25,000-30,000. Committee agreed they would not pay for it but would approve installation if Mount Olive covers costs. Motion by Olsen that if Mount Olive agrees to cover costs, the committee recommends board approval. Seconded by Sherwanka. Passed unanimously. Michael will contact Mount Olive.

Fire Chief explained a new regional program where Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston fire departments can inspect food trucks with a sticker valid across all participating municipalities. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Fire Marshal Marty already inspected one truck that failed due to improper LP piping (galvanized steel) and unsecured propane cylinders. Motion by Sherwanka to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing corrections: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence near an apartment building, and 2211 Radkey Avenue duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt noted an additional issue with Weston Villa Apartments having eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion made and seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization for a raze order on a structure abandoned since 2010-2011, posted since 2021. A fire department training burn was previously attempted but the structure was deemed too unsafe. Current owners are up to date on taxes and prefer the village proceed with demolition. Costs will be added to the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at a park, predicting the grass will be destroyed by June and noting that parking on grass appears to violate village code. He recommended relocating ADA parking spots from grass to adjacent asphalt for wheelchair accessibility. He questioned the new fire inspection requirement for food trucks, asking where it originated since no trucks have burned down. He raised concerns about mobile food vendor regulations on public property and interpretation of farmers market days. Finally, he criticized lack of accountability on long-standing code violations, noting some cases are very old with no visible progress.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended for board of trustees approval
E-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions recommended for board approval
Michael from Public Works to identify 6-8 specific areas for winter parking restrictions and return in July
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections recommended for approval
Address corrections for three properties recommended for board approval
Raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue authorized to proceed
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
